scope: Defensive audit of backup / disaster-recovery (BACKUP) and data-retention enforcement (RETAIN). Phase 11 shipped a fail-closed production-config validator and deploy automation; Phase 12 closes the next layer — what happens when the database goes away (BACKUP), and what happens to data that should age out (RETAIN). Both are direct DPA / GDPR exposure: an unrecoverable data loss event AND data that lingers past its retention period are equally regulator-flagged.

previous_audits: Phase 11 (phase-11-audit-prd.json): 21 DEPLOY/SECRETS findings closed across 6 commits
context_baseline.do_not_reflag: Findings duplicating items already closed in phases 1-11. SECRETS-9 already shipped a key-rotation runbook; this phase does not re-flag rotation procedure. ArchiveOldPayments already implements payment archival; RETAIN findings target the OTHER tables without archival.

qualityStandards.test: All Phase-12 fixes must pass `php artisan test --parallel` and `vendor/bin/pint --test`.
qualityStandards.ci: Each commit must land on main with green CI before the next phase starts.
qualityStandards.no_breaking_change: Adding retention pruning commands must be gated on --confirm (mirror webhook:rotate-secret). Pruning is destructive.

BACKUP.description: Backup / disaster-recovery posture — production-database backup automation, backup verification (a backup is only as good as the last restore drill), backup retention, document-storage durability, encrypted-column key escrow, documented RPO/RTO. The repo today has ZERO backup automation; loss of the production DB cluster equals total data loss.

RETAIN.description: Data-retention enforcement — the codebase DEFINES retention windows (AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS, SECURITY_LOG_RETENTION_DAYS, DATA_RETENTION_YEARS, DELETION_GRACE_DAYS) and KenyaDpaService documents them, but very little actually ENFORCES them. Audit/security logs grow unboundedly; the gdpr:process-deletions command exists but is not scheduled; soft-deleted rows never force-delete past grace. DPA and GDPR Article 17 exposure.

items.title: No backup mechanism exists — DB cluster loss equals total data loss
items.evidence: grep composer.json for 'backup' returns nothing. No mysqldump cron in console.php. No S3 sync. No host-level systemd timer documented anywhere in repo. The only backup story is whatever's configured on the DB host out-of-band — invisible to the app's deploy and audit process.
items.fix: Install spatie/laravel-backup. Configure config/backup.php with: mysqldump-style DB dump, optional file-tree backup (storage/app uploads), encrypted disk target (s3 with KMS), per-environment naming. Add a daily schedule + a weekly schedule. Notify failures via Sentry (OBS-1) + email. Document the package in README.

items.title: No backup verification — a backup is only as good as the last restore drill
items.evidence: Even after BACKUP-1 ships, there is no automated assurance the backups are RESTORABLE. A backup that silently encrypts to garbage (wrong AWS region, corrupted gzip, schema-incompatible) gives false confidence. spatie/laravel-backup's monitor command checks size and age but does NOT actually restore.
items.fix: Add `php artisan backup:verify` command that: pulls the latest backup, restores to a throwaway DB schema, runs a smoke-test query (SELECT COUNT(*) from leases / invoices / users), drops the schema. Schedule weekly. Failure -> Sentry + email.

items.title: No backup retention policy — accumulate forever or age out unmanaged
items.evidence: Without explicit retention, two failure modes: (a) backups accumulate forever, S3 cost grows without bound; (b) backups age out via host TTL with no policy, data loss horizon is whatever the host happens to do. Neither is auditable.
items.fix: In the new config/backup.php, configure GFS retention: daily for 14 days, weekly for 12 weeks, monthly for 24 months, yearly for 7 years (matches DATA_RETENTION_YEARS). Document the policy in README + docs/runbooks/.

items.title: No documented RPO / RTO — operators don't know what's acceptable
items.evidence: RPO (Recovery Point Objective: how much data loss is acceptable) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective: how fast must we recover) are the two numbers DR strategy is built on. Neither is documented. Without them, BACKUP-1/2/3 ship without a target to validate against.
items.fix: Add docs/runbooks/disaster-recovery.md documenting RPO (proposed: 24h for now, shrink to 1h once binlog-based PITR is wired) and RTO (proposed: 4h cold restore, validated by quarterly drills). Include the restore procedure, the rollback contacts, and the post-incident review template.

items.title: Document storage on FILESYSTEM_DISK has no documented versioning / lifecycle
items.evidence: Lease documents, KYC photos, payment proofs all live on FILESYSTEM_DISK. If the configured disk is S3 without versioning enabled (the default), a delete (intentional or accidental) is permanent. A misconfigured bucket lifecycle could also expire objects unexpectedly.
items.fix: Document the required S3 bucket settings (versioning ENABLED, MFA-delete on production, lifecycle policy that matches RETAIN-* retention windows). Add a validator check that warns if FILESYSTEM_DISK is 'local' in production. Optional: a `php artisan storage:audit` command that walks each disk's config and reports gaps.

items.title: No APP_KEY escrow for encrypted columns in backups
items.evidence: Backups contain rows whose encrypted columns (payment configs, KYC, 2FA secrets) are encrypted with the APP_KEY that was current AT BACKUP TIME. If APP_KEY is rotated AND the old key is destroyed AND a backup from before the rotation is restored, the encrypted columns are unrecoverable. CryptRotate handles forward rotation but not the escrow problem.
items.fix: Update key-rotation.md with an APP_KEY ESCROW section: before rotating, snapshot the old key to a sealed secret store (1Password vault, AWS KMS-encrypted file, etc.) labelled with the rotation date. Retention: as long as the oldest backup that needs it. Document the unsealing procedure for restoration.

items.title: Redis state (sessions, queue, cache) has no backup story
items.evidence: .env.production.example proposes QUEUE_CONNECTION=redis. On Redis failover, in-flight jobs are lost. Sessions are also Redis-backed (CACHE_STORE=redis); a flush logs every user out. No documented Redis backup or replica setup.
items.fix: Document Redis durability tier requirements: AOF persistence enabled, replica node for failover, AWS ElastiCache with automatic backup retention if that's the deploy target. Add to README's Deployment section.

items.title: No restore-drill cadence — last successful restore is unknown
items.evidence: DR posture is only credible if recently exercised. Without a documented drill cadence and a log of past drills, the 'we have backups' claim is untested.
items.fix: Add docs/runbooks/dr-drill-log.md template. Mandate a quarterly drill: restore the latest backup to staging, run a 10-query smoke test, record date + duration + observed RPO/RTO in the log. The cron-reminder approach used for APP_KEY rotation applies here too.

items.title: AUDIT_RETENTION_DAYS=365 defined but no command prunes audit_logs
items.evidence: config('security.audit.retention_days') = 365 but grep finds zero callers OTHER than the config reading itself. The audit_logs table grows unboundedly forever. Storage cost + Kenya DPA hazard ('storage limitation' principle: personal data shall not be kept longer than necessary).
items.fix: Add `php artisan logs:prune --table=audit --dry-run/--confirm` that DELETEs rows older than retention_days. Schedule daily at 03:30. SecurityLog entry per prune run with deleted-count metadata. Test: insert N+1 rows past retention, run command with --confirm, assert N pruned + 1 fresh row remains.

items.title: SECURITY_LOG_RETENTION_DAYS=90 defined but no command prunes security_logs
items.evidence: Same pattern as RETAIN-1 but for the security_logs table (login events, password changes, impersonation, etc.). config('security.logging.retention_days') = 90 with zero consumers.
items.fix: Extend the same `logs:prune` command from RETAIN-1 with --table=security support. Schedule alongside the audit prune.

items.title: gdpr:process-deletions command exists but is not scheduled
items.evidence: ProcessScheduledDeletions implements GDPR Article 17 deletion processing (after the DELETION_GRACE_DAYS window). routes/console.php does NOT schedule it. So a user who requested deletion has their account marked, never actually deleted. GDPR fine territory.
items.fix: Add `$schedule('gdpr:process-deletions', fn ($e) => $e->dailyAt('02:45'))` to routes/console.php. Use the existing $schedule helper that already wraps onOneServer + onFailure handling.

items.title: Soft-deleted rows are never force-deleted past grace period
items.evidence: 15+ models use SoftDeletes (Building, Lease, Invoice, Property, Unit, etc.). 'Deleting' marks deleted_at; the row stays forever. DELETION_GRACE_DAYS=30 is defined but unused. A user who deletes a lease still has the lease data on disk in perpetuity.
items.fix: Add `php artisan soft-deleted:purge --grace-days=30 --dry-run/--confirm`. Iterates known soft-deletable models, force-deletes rows where deleted_at < now() - graceDays. Per-model audit log entry summarizing count. Schedule daily.

items.title: KenyaDpaService documents retention windows but service does not enforce them
items.evidence: getDataRetentionRequirements() returns a static array: lease=7y, financial=7y, consent=3y after withdrawal, communications=1y, etc. The values are returned for display only — no scheduled job reads them and enforces.
items.fix: Rewrite KenyaDpaService::getDataRetentionRequirements to be the source-of-truth that drives a `php artisan dpa:enforce-retention` command. Each requirement maps to a model + scope + retention period; the command iterates, force-deletes rows past their window. Audit log entry per run. Reference KenyaDpaService::DATA_CATEGORIES_PURGED in the audit_log metadata.

items.title: DataExportService produces files but no scheduled cleanup of old exports
items.evidence: DataExportService::cleanup() exists (the comment 'Clean up old exports' suggests it). No scheduled caller. So export files accumulate on FILESYSTEM_DISK — each export contains tenant PII bundled for download.
items.fix: Verify DataExportService::cleanup is wired; if not, schedule it daily at 03:15. Default retention: 7 days post-download (long enough for the user to actually retrieve).

items.title: WebhookDeadLetter has no documented retention — PII accumulates forever
items.evidence: Failed payment webhook payloads are persisted to webhook_dead_letters for manual reconciliation. Payloads contain phone numbers, account numbers, transaction IDs. Without retention, the table is an ever-growing PII archive.
items.fix: Add a 'resolved_at' / 'archived_at' lifecycle. A scheduled command prunes rows where resolved_at < now() - 90 days. Unresolved old rows escalate to an ops alert instead of silently aging out.

items.title: config('payments.retention_days')=28 — investigate consumer, document or remove
items.evidence: config/payments.php defines retention_days=28 with no clear consumer in grep. Could be dead config (worth removing to avoid confusion) or used by a service I missed (worth documenting).
items.fix: grep all callers of config('payments.retention_days'). If used, add a docblock on the consumer pointing back to the config key + the retention reason. If unused, remove the config entry.

items.title: failed_jobs table grows unboundedly — failed-jobs-growth-monitor only alerts
items.evidence: failed-jobs-growth-monitor (added in Phase-5 OBS-13) ALERTS when failed_jobs grows past a threshold but does not PRUNE. A flaky integration that intermittently fails fills the table; alert fatigue follows.
items.fix: Add `php artisan queue:prune-failed --hours=720` (built-in Laravel command) to the schedule. Daily at 03:45. Operators retain 30 days of failed-job history for debugging.

items.title: webhook_logs / bank_webhook_logs grow unboundedly
items.evidence: Every M-Pesa, Paystack, IntaSend, Coop, Equity, KCB webhook hit lands in one of these tables (Phase-5 OBS observability work). High volume tables. Phase-5 CRYPTO-11 encrypts payloads — encryption doesn't shrink them. Without retention, primary-key index bloat slows reads.
items.fix: Extend the `logs:prune` command from RETAIN-1/2 with --table=webhook and --table=bank-webhook support. Default retention: 180 days (longer than payment-reconciliation operator workflows need).

phase_1.title: Critical: install backup automation + retention pruning that already has DEFINED windows
phase_1.rationale: Highest-leverage cluster. BACKUP-1 closes the catastrophic-loss exposure; RETAIN-1/2/3 close the easy wins where config + service code already exists, we just need the scheduled enforcement.

phase_2.title: Critical: backup verification + RPO/RTO documentation + soft-delete force-purge
phase_2.rationale: BACKUP-2/3/4 turn 'we have backups' into 'we have AUDITABLE backups'. RETAIN-4 closes the soft-delete-eternity bug class.

phase_3.title: Medium: storage durability + DPA enforcement wiring + dead-letter retention
phase_3.scope: BACKUP-5, BACKUP-6, BACKUP-7, RETAIN-5, RETAIN-6, RETAIN-7, RETAIN-8
phase_3.rationale: Polish + the longer-tail retention sweeps. Each individually small.

phase_4.title: LOW: high-volume table prunes + drill cadence

out_of_scope: Kenya DPA / GDPR full coverage (consent UX, breach notification, export completeness) — own phase
Dependency supply-chain audit beyond CVE bumps — own phase
